variables.content: ARPES single-particle spectral-function measurements at finite experimental energy resolution produce a quasiparticle peak whose linewidth is determined by combined instrumental broadening, thermal disorder, and intrinsic many-body scattering; in CsPbBr$_3$ the relevant optical longitudinal-optical (LO) phonon energies are \\leq25\\ \\mathrm{meV} and phonon-replica satellites separated by these energies cannot be resolved because the experimental quasiparticle linewidth exceeds the mode energy separations, yet the ARPES-measured quasiparticle dispersion and the fitted increase in effective mass still faithfully reflect the quasiparticle mass renormalization due to electron–phonon interactions, so that extraction of an increased effective mass from the fitted dispersion constitutes a valid signature of polaronic mass dressing even in the absence of resolved replica peaks.

The sequence consisting of (i) ab initio DFPT evaluation of long-wavelength phonons, Born charges, and dielectric response and computation of the long-wavelength Fröhlich vertex and its energy-resolved squared coupling density d(g^{2})/d\\omega to obtain the effective single-mode parameters \\tilde{g}_{\\mathrm{LO}}^{2} and \\hbar\\tilde{\\omega}_{\\mathrm{LO}}, and (ii) a Feynman-polaron calculation using the ab initio bare mass m_{0} and the Fröhlich parameter \\alpha computed from those inputs, reproduces the ARPES-measured hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e for orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ within experimental uncertainty; therefore this ab initio electron–phonon evaluation combined with a Feynman-polaron treatment is a viable predictive method to compute carrier effective masses in lead-halide perovskites when the Fröhlich mechanism dominates carrier dressing.

Cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr_{3}) undergoes crystallographic phase changes with temperature and adopts an orthorhombic ground-state structure at room temperature, yet ARPES band dispersions measured at room temperature can in practice resemble higher-symmetry cubic-phase dispersions for some momentum-space cuts; therefore using an orthorhombic-structure G_{0}W_{0} calculation that yields m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} for direct quantitative comparison with room-temperature ARPES data assumes that the orthorhombic theoretical band structure is an appropriate representation of the electronic states probed in the ARPES experiment, and further assumes that any difference in structural symmetry between the theoretical model and the actual electronic-state symmetry sampled by ARPES does not change the effective mass by an amount comparable to the experimental–theoretical difference being evaluated.

The many-body FEP-DMC calculation of the polaron quasiparticle dispersion in crystalline anatase TiO2 yields a polaron band that is noticeably flatter than the single-particle electronic quasiparticle dispersion obtained from a G0W0 calculation (where G0W0 denotes one-shot GW quasiparticle corrections on top of density functional theory), and extracting effective masses from the curvature of these dispersions along the Γ–X high-symmetry direction gives a polaron effective-mass m_p that is larger than the electronic effective-mass m_e with an enhancement factor m_p/m_e = 1.6, consistent with intermediate electron–phonon renormalization characteristic of a large, dispersive polaron.

Polaron‑parameter calculations performed with variational polaron theory and material dielectric and effective‑mass inputs yield an estimated polaron radius r_p ≈ 36 Å for CsPbBr3; using the approximate mapping between polaron spatial extent and the carrier‑density threshold for overlap n_{\mathrm{overlap}}\sim 1/r_p^3 (modulo order‑unity packing factors), the larger r_p in CsPbBr3 reduces n_{\mathrm{overlap}} and thereby increases the propensity for spatial overlap of distinct polarons and for emitted optical phonons from different polarons to be reabsorbed, which enhances the density‑dependent hot‑phonon bottleneck observed experimentally in CsPbBr3.

The momentum-resolved valence-band dispersion of single-crystalline orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ at room temperature was measured by angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (ARPES); here the measured quasiparticle energy as a function of the in-plane crystal momentum was fitted near the valence-band maximum (VBM) by the isotropic in-plane parabolic dispersion E(k)=E_{\\mathrm{VBM}}+\\hbar^{2}k^{2}/(2m_{\\mathrm{exp}}), where k is the magnitude of the two-dimensional in-plane wave vector (units Å^{-1}), \\hbar is the reduced Planck constant, and m_e is the free electron mass, and the energy zero was set at the VBM defined as the energy of the maximum of the energy-distribution curve (EDC) at the band top; this fit, performed on the upper valence band along the surface high-symmetry direction labeled \\bar{\\Gamma}-\\bar{M}, returns an experimental hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e.

The electronic conduction-band effective mass m_e along the Γ–X direction in anatase TiO2, used as the reference to compute the polaron mass enhancement factor m_p/m_e, is taken from a G0W0 quasiparticle calculation performed with the PBESol DFT starting point, a plane-wave basis, 500 bands included in the polarization with the Bruneval–Gonze terminator, and Wannier interpolation of the quasiparticle dispersion; the proposition asserts that with these computational parameters the residual G0W0 errors in m_e are small compared to the uncertainty in the reported polaron mass enhancement.

The effective mass entering the Drude prefactor used to infer transport parameters for photogenerated carriers in CsPbBr3 is taken as m_p = 3.5 × m*, where m_p is the effective polaron mass and m* = 0.21 m_e is the bare electronic band effective mass (m_e = 9.10938356×10^−31 kg is the electron rest mass); the multiplicative factor 3.5 is adopted from Feynman–Osaka polaron model estimates to account for polaron mass enhancement, and using this fixed scaling produces a physically appropriate value of m_p for the photogenerated carrier population in the studied nanocrystal thin films such that the uncertainty in this adopted scaling is small compared with the quoted mobility estimate μ = q τ_s / m_p.

In room-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr3, the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum (VBM) located at the bulk Brillouin-zone R point was determined by fitting local quadratic dispersions and found to be m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0}, where m_{0} denotes the free-electron mass; this reported value is the average of four independent measurements on four different cleaved samples that produced individual fitted masses of 0.19\,m_{0}, 0.23\,m_{0}, 0.20\,m_{0}, and 0.19\,m_{0}. The identification of the VBM at the bulk R point was made by photon-energy-dependent ARPES mapping of the perpendicular crystal momentum k_{\perp}, and under the free-electron final-state mapping k_{\perp}=\sqrt{\tfrac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}}\bigl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}\theta + V_{0}\bigr)} with inner potential parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} (where \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} the photoelectron kinetic energy referenced to the vacuum level, and \theta the electron emission polar angle), the first R point in these measurements corresponds to photon energy h\nu=29\ \mathrm{eV}.

Single-shot $G_{0}W_{0}$ quasiparticle calculations performed on top of Kohn–Sham orbitals obtained from the HSE06 hybrid density functional including spin–orbit coupling (SOC) and with a tuned fraction $\alpha$ of exact Hartree–Fock exchange produce direct bandgaps (conduction-band minimum and valence-band maximum located at the $R$-point of the cubic Brillouin zone, $R=(0.5,0.5,0.5)$) of $E_{g}=2.37\ \mathrm{eV}$ for MAPbBr$_{3}$ with $\alpha=50\%$, $E_{g}=2.27\ \mathrm{eV}$ for FAPbBr$_{3}$ with $\alpha=40\%$, and $E_{g}=2.30\ \mathrm{eV}$ for CsPbBr$_{3}$ with $\alpha=55\%$; parabolic fits to the $G_{0}W_{0}$ quasiparticle band dispersions including SOC in small $k$-space regions around the band extrema yield electron and hole effective masses whose harmonic combination $\mu^{-1}=m_{e}^{*-1}+m_{h}^{*-1}$ gives reduced masses $\mu$ (in units of the free-electron mass $m_{0}$) of $\mu=0.113$ for MAPbBr$_{3}$, $\mu=0.115$ for FAPbBr$_{3}$, and $\mu=0.080$ for CsPbBr$_{3}$.
